charts.title: Money Supply
charts.subtitle: M2 to GDP ratio, percent
charts.description: M2 measures the money supply including cash, checking deposits, savings deposits, and money market funds. This chart shows M2 as a share of nominal GDP.

charts.title: Real GDP per Capita
charts.subtitle: USD, in thousands
charts.description: Real GDP per capita divides inflation-adjusted gross domestic product by population. It is a broad measure of average economic output per person.

charts.title: Federal Funds Rate
charts.subtitle: Effective rate, percent
charts.description: The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which banks lend reserves to each other overnight. It is the Federal Reserve's primary tool for conducting monetary policy.

charts.title: Yield Spread
charts.subtitle: 2-year Treasury minus fed funds rate, percentage points
charts.description: The spread between the 2-year Treasury yield and the effective federal funds rate. A negative spread suggests markets expect the Fed to cut rates. A positive spread suggests rates may rise or that markets see policy as accommodative. Full history shows monthly averages; recent 3 years switches to weekly.

charts.title: Productivity
charts.subtitle: GDP per hour of work, 2023
charts.description: Labor productivity measures GDP per hour worked, reflecting how efficiently an economy converts labor into output. Higher productivity supports higher wages and living standards.

charts.title: CPI Inflation Rate
charts.subtitle: Year-over-year change, percent
charts.description: The Consumer Price Index measures the average change in prices paid by urban consumers for a basket of goods and services. All Items includes everything; Core excludes volatile food and energy prices. Both show year-over-year percent change.

charts.title: Trade Balance
charts.subtitle: Goods and services, share of GDP, percent
charts.description: The trade balance is exports minus imports of goods and services, shown here as a share of GDP. A negative value indicates a trade deficit.

charts.title: Tariff Rate
charts.subtitle: Tariff revenue as share of goods imports, percent
charts.description: The effective tariff rate is calculated as customs duties collected divided by the value of goods imports. It reflects the average tax rate on imported goods.

charts.title: Exchange Rates
charts.subtitle: Trade-weighted dollar index, Jan 2006 = 100
charts.description: Trade-weighted exchange rate indices published by the Federal Reserve. The Broad index covers all major US trading partners. The Major index covers advanced economies only. A higher value means a stronger dollar. Full history shows monthly averages; recent 3 years switches to weekly.

charts.title: Employment Rate
charts.subtitle: Employed share of population, percent
charts.description: The employment-population ratio shows the share of the population that is employed. Prime age (25–54) excludes younger adults in school and older adults who may be retired. The all-ages rate (16+) includes everyone. Unlike the unemployment rate, neither falls when discouraged workers stop looking for jobs.

charts.title: Industrial Production
charts.subtitle: Manufacturing output, index, 2012 = 100
charts.description: The industrial production index measures the real output of the manufacturing sector. It is published by the Federal Reserve and indexed to 2012. A value above 100 indicates higher output than in 2012.

charts.title: Unemployment Rate
charts.subtitle: Unemployed share of labor force, percent
charts.description: The unemployment rate is the share of the labor force that is actively seeking work but unable to find a job. It is one of the most widely followed indicators of labor market conditions.

charts.title: Job Openings
charts.subtitle: Job openings per unemployed person
charts.description: This ratio divides the number of job openings (from the JOLTS survey) by the number of unemployed persons. A value above 1.0 means there are more job openings than unemployed persons. A higher ratio indicates a tighter labor market with more opportunities per job seeker.

charts.title: Weekly Earnings
charts.subtitle: Full-time workers, inflation-adjusted USD
charts.description: Real usual weekly earnings at key percentiles of the full-time wage distribution, adjusted for regional inflation using CPS microdata. The 10th percentile (P10) means 90% of full-time workers earn more. Toggle series in the legend to compare across the distribution.

charts.title: Federal Receipts
charts.subtitle: Total receipts, trailing 12 months, percent of GDP
charts.description: Total federal government receipts (taxes, fees, and other revenue) over a trailing 12-month period, expressed as a share of GDP. This smooths seasonal variation in tax collections.

charts.title: Poverty Rate
charts.subtitle: Share of population below 50% of median income, 2022
charts.description: The relative poverty rate measures the share of population living below 50% of the national median disposable income. It allows cross-country comparison of income adequacy.

charts.title: House Price Index
charts.subtitle: Index, 1991 = 100
charts.description: The FHFA House Price Index tracks changes in single-family home prices based on repeat sales data from mortgages purchased or guaranteed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

charts.title: CPI Components
charts.subtitle: Year-over-year price change by category, percent
charts.description: Year-over-year price changes for nine major spending categories. Shelter includes rent and owners' equivalent rent. Vehicles combines new and used cars weighted by spending share. Other includes apparel, education, communication, personal care, and household furnishings.

charts.title: Government Spending
charts.subtitle: General government spending, percent of GDP, 2023
charts.description: Total general government expenditure (federal, state, and local) as a share of GDP. This includes spending on social programs, defense, infrastructure, and public services.

charts.title: Output and Wages
charts.subtitle: Inflation-adjusted USD, in thousands
charts.description: This chart compares inflation-adjusted GDP per worker with median earnings (annualized from usual weekly earnings), both in thousands of dollars. The gap between the two lines reflects how broadly economic gains are shared.

charts.title: Corporate Profits
charts.subtitle: Share of net national income, percent
charts.description: Corporate profits before tax as a share of net national income. Profits are adjusted to exclude inventory price gains and use consistent depreciation. Net national income excludes depreciation, which isn't received as income.

charts.title: Business Investment
charts.subtitle: Net fixed investment, percent of GDP
charts.description: Private nonresidential fixed investment minus depreciation, as a share of GDP. This measures net additions to the economy's productive capital stock, including equipment, structures, and intellectual property.

charts.title: Oil Price
charts.subtitle: WTI crude oil, dollars per barrel
charts.description: West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil spot price in dollars per barrel. WTI is the primary benchmark for US oil prices. Full history shows monthly averages; recent 3 years switches to weekly.

charts.title: Cash Flow
charts.subtitle: Magnificent 7 quarterly operating cash flow, share of GDP, percent
charts.description: Quarterly operating cash flow for the seven largest US technology companies (Apple, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, NVIDIA, Meta, Tesla), each shown as a share of quarterly GDP. Operating cash flow measures the cash generated by core business operations.

charts.title: Sectoral Balances
charts.subtitle: Net lending/borrowing by sector, share of GDP, percent
charts.description: The three sectoral financial balances — private, government, and rest of world — must sum to zero by accounting identity. A government deficit (negative) is offset by private surplus and/or foreign capital inflows. Data shows net lending (+) or borrowing (−) as a share of GDP.
